title: Chapter 1
html:
Foreword

William Wycherley’s The Country Wife emerges from the incandescent and often scandalous blaze of the Restoration stage, where wit, libertinism, and satiric precision commingled in a manner both shocking and liberating to a culture just released from Puritan austerity. To read this play is to enter a world where language itself is weaponized: a dazzling carnival of insinuation, innuendo, and verbal dueling that mirrors the volatile social experiment of Charles II’s court. The theatre had been closed for nearly two decades under Cromwell’s rule, and when the playhouses reopened, they did not return chastened but rather with an exuberant vengeance. Wycherley belongs to this moment as its most caustic genius, exposing hypocrisy through the merciless energies of laughter.

The Restoration comedy of manners, of which The Country Wife is exemplary and notorious, delights in its paradoxes: a genre of refinement concerned with the grotesqueries of refinement, of civility addicted to the exposure of false civility. Wycherley excels in this dialectic, crafting a dramatic texture that is simultaneously urbane and brutal, aristocratic and vulgar, decorous in wit but indecorous in matter. That very doubleness places him within the great tradition of Western comedy, alongside Aristophanes, Plautus, and Molière, all of whom understood that laughter serves as the most unflinching critic of human pretension. The Restoration stage, however, inflected this ancient comic wisdom with a peculiarly English cynicism, one sharpened by political upheaval, religious strife, and the carnal appetites of a restored monarchy.

The greatness of Wycherley is inseparable from his audacity. The Country Wife remains infamous for its erotic frankness, for its unabashed delight in scandal. Yet its endurance lies not in prurience but in the brilliance with which Wycherley transforms scandal into form. The play is built upon linguistic invention: puns and double meanings that hover at the threshold between wit and obscenity. This verbal daring is what grants the work its aesthetic and canonical power. For all its notorious indecency, it is not smut but style that ensures its immortality. Wycherley’s dialogue achieves the high art of Restoration repartee, a music of malice and delight in which each line strikes like a rapier.

One cannot situate Wycherley apart from the moral ambivalence of his age. The Restoration court reveled in licentiousness, but its plays never abandon judgment; indeed, they thrive upon it. The Country Wife is less a hymn to libertinism than a merciless satire upon it. The so-called hero embodies an amoral cunning that delights even as it repels, and in this doubleness Wycherley anticipates later masters of the ironic hero, from Byron’s Don Juan to Nabokov’s Humbert Humbert. What Wycherley dramatizes is not sexual freedom alone, but the hypocrisy that surrounds it—the prudish mask of society and the incessant strategies through which individuals negotiate appearances. In this he is a moralist of a peculiarly severe order, though his morality manifests itself obliquely, through wit’s scalpel rather than sermon’s hammer.

Comparisons to Molière are inevitable, for both dramatists anatomize hypocrisy. But whereas Molière tempers satire with a Gallic geniality, Wycherley is pitiless, corrosive, more akin to Swift in his relentless vision of folly. He strips away the consolations of reform or redemption. In The Country Wife, laughter does not cleanse but exposes; it does not heal but illuminates. This implacability is what has often unsettled audiences and critics alike, yet it is also the mark of canonical strength. Works that console easily are soon forgotten; those that disturb endure.

Wycherley’s stylistic genius resides in compression. His dialogue is crystalline, each word edged with significance, often with two or three significances at once. The Restoration theatre cherished speed of wit, and Wycherley delivers a velocity unmatched even among his contemporaries. He anticipates Wilde’s epigrammatic brilliance, though Wilde is lush where Wycherley is austere, Wilde indulgent in beauty where Wycherley remains severe in exposure. If Wilde is the jeweler of wit, Wycherley is its surgeon, cutting always to the quick.

To consider the play in the vast expanse of the Western tradition is to note its dialogue with both Aristophanic comedy and Shakespearian farce. Wycherley inherits the bawdy exuberance of Falstaff and the linguistic play of Beatrice and Benedick, but he strips these of Shakespeare’s genial expansiveness, leaving only a distilled essence of wit sharpened into cruelty. If Shakespeare reconciles, Wycherley dissects. Yet the act of dissection itself becomes revelatory. The Restoration stage was perhaps the first to recognize that the social world is itself a theatre of masks, that human intercourse is always already performative. In this way, Wycherley anticipates modernity, preparing the ground for Ibsen, Shaw, and Pirandello. His play is less a relic of libertine London than an inaugural document of the drama of appearances that has dominated theatre ever since.

The canonical significance of The Country Wife is further secured by its philosophical undercurrent: its recognition of desire as the irreducible energy of social existence. The Restoration comic world is governed by appetite, by the restless pursuit of pleasure, power, and position. Wycherley renders this not as pathology but as ontology; his characters do not so much choose desire as incarnate it. In this sense, he belongs to the lineage of writers—from Lucretius to Freud—who have understood eros as the root of both comedy and tragedy, of folly and of wisdom. If Aristophanes in The Symposium portrays eros as the divine search for completion, Wycherley portrays it as the irrepressible compulsion that dismantles every façade of propriety.

The endurance of The Country Wife lies in its refusal of sentimentality. It is not a moral comedy in the later eighteenth-century sense, nor does it flatter bourgeois virtues as Sheridan would. It remains obstinately aristocratic, even savage, in its worldview. Its laughter is the laughter of those who understand that the world is governed by duplicity, that virtue is often a mask worn by vice. Such laughter can be disconcerting to later ages that demand edification from art, but it is precisely this resistance to edification that grants the play its vitality. The canon is preserved not by moral instruction but by aesthetic power, and Wycherley’s play retains an aesthetic voltage that continues to shock.

To read Wycherley today is to encounter both distance and intimacy. Distance, because the social codes of Restoration London—its sexual mores, its aristocratic manners, its gender dynamics—are alien to us. Intimacy, because the exposure of hypocrisy, the dramatization of desire, the endless play of appearances remain our own. Indeed, in a world where image and façade dominate public life, Wycherley’s ruthless comedy feels prophetic. His characters are masters of performance, their lives a ceaseless theatre of deception. The Restoration stage, in this sense, anticipates the modern condition, where identity itself is staged and sincerity always compromised.

Thus the canonical importance of The Country Wife is not confined to its historical moment but extends into the structure of modernity itself. Wycherley exposes the eternal doubleness of human beings: the ceaseless oscillation between appearance and reality, desire and restraint, wit and folly. His play belongs to the lineage of works that refuse to flatter us, that show us as we are rather than as we wish to be. Like Swift’s Gulliver’s Travels, like Flaubert’s Madame Bovary, like Ibsen’s A Doll’s House, it holds a mirror that reveals not our virtues but our vanities.

What then is the enduring relevance of this scandalous comedy? It lies in the recognition that art which offends often preserves. The outrages of Wycherley’s age are not ours, yet their exposure of duplicity remains urgent. In a cultural climate that oscillates between puritan censoriousness and libertine permissiveness, The Country Wife continues to remind us that wit is the most enduring form of critique. It is not decorum but candor, not sermon but laughter, that endures. To laugh with Wycherley is to accept the unmasking of the self, the recognition that hypocrisy is not a vice of the few but the condition of all.

Wycherley’s originality, then, is not in the libertinism alone but in the artistic discipline with which he renders it. He transforms scandal into structure, desire into dialogue, hypocrisy into form. His play is not merely a historical curiosity of a licentious age, but a canonical monument of dramatic literature: dense with wit, ruthless in satire, and profound in its exposure of human folly. If Bloom’s dictum holds that the canon is formed by works that generate fresh interpretation endlessly, then The Country Wife is secure, for it remains inexhaustible in its capacity to provoke, unsettle, and illuminate.

We read it not to learn morality but to learn language, not to be comforted but to be awakened. Wycherley stands among the immortal comedians of the West, a master whose laughter is the most unsparing of truths. To enter The Country Wife is to enter the theatre of human duplicity, where wit is both weapon and revelation, and where laughter, however scandalous, proves finally to be wisdom.
